locked_facts: Adiabatic: Q = 0 (no heat exchange with surroundings)
ΔU = -W: work done by gas comes from internal energy
Adiabatic curve is STEEPER than isothermal on PV diagram
truth_statements: In an adiabatic process, no heat is exchanged between system and surroundings
The work done during adiabatic expansion comes at the expense of internal energy
An adiabatic expansion causes the temperature to decrease
The adiabatic curve on a PV diagram is steeper than the isothermal curve
PV to the power gamma is constant in a reversible adiabatic process

aha_moment.what_to_show: PV diagram overlaying both isothermal (dashed) and adiabatic (solid) curves from same starting point. Adiabatic is steeper. Temperature readout drops visibly. Energy bars: Q=0, ΔU negative (energy lost), W positive (work done). The gas cooled itself by doing work!
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Particles slow down visibly as piston expands. Temperature number decreasing in real-time. No heat arrow (Q=0). Yet piston moved — work came from the gas's own thermal energy.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see temperature DROP despite no heat leaving. The internal energy went directly to work. No heat exchange needed for temperature change.

simulation_states.label: Hot compressed gas — insulated walls
simulation_states.description: Gas at 400K, 200kPa, 3L. Cylinder walls are insulated (shown with hatching). Particles moving fast. No heat arrows anywhere — the system is thermally isolated.
simulation_states.emphasis: The walls are insulated: Q = 0 throughout. No heat can enter or leave. Watch what happens when gas expands.

simulation_states.label: Adiabatic expansion — gas cools!
simulation_states.description: Piston moves outward. Particles visibly slow down. Temperature drops from 400K toward 280K. No heat arrow — the energy for work came from the gas itself! PV curve traces steep path.
simulation_states.emphasis: The gas is COOLING as it expands — no heat is leaving, but temperature drops. Where did the energy go? Into pushing the piston!

simulation_states.label: Steeper than isothermal — the comparison
simulation_states.description: PV diagram shows adiabatic curve (solid, steeper) alongside isothermal curve (dashed, less steep) from same starting point. Energy bars: Q=0, ΔU<0, W>0. Temperature readout shows final lower value.
simulation_states.emphasis: AHA: Adiabatic curve is STEEPER than isothermal. Gas ends up at lower temperature AND lower pressure for same final volume. No heat needed for temperature to change!

simulation_states.label: Explore — adiabatic vs isothermal
simulation_states.description: Both curves visible on PV diagram. Energy bars: Q=0, ΔU = -W. All labels visible. Caption: PVγ = constant, W = (P₁V₁ - P₂V₂)/(γ-1).
simulation_states.emphasis: In adiabatic expansion: Q=0, temperature drops, work comes from internal energy. The steeper PV curve means less work than isothermal for same volume change.

trigger_keywords: Q=0 toh temperature change nahi hoga
adiabatic mein temp same rehta hai
bina heat ke temperature kaise change hoga
common_misconceptions.student_belief: If no heat is exchanged (Q=0), temperature cannot change
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Temperature CAN change without heat exchange! In an adiabatic expansion, the gas does work on the surroundings. Since Q=0, by the first law: ΔU = -W. The work energy comes from the gas's internal energy, which decreases, causing temperature to drop. Similarly, adiabatic compression increases temperature (like a bicycle pump getting hot).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show insulated walls (Q=0 prominently displayed), piston expanding, and temperature readout decreasing in real-time. Particles visibly slow down. Energy bars: Q=0, W>0, ΔU<0.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: Adiabatic and isothermal curves look the same on a PV diagram
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Adiabatic curves are STEEPER than isothermal curves on a PV diagram. This is because γ > 1 (always), so PVγ = constant drops pressure faster than PV = constant for the same volume increase. For the same expansion, adiabatic process does LESS work (smaller area under curve) than isothermal.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Overlay both curves from the same starting point. The adiabatic curve clearly bends down more steeply. Shade the areas to show isothermal work > adiabatic work.

typical_question_types: find final temperature after adiabatic expansion
compare work done: isothermal vs adiabatic
common_traps: forgetting that adiabatic expansion COOLS the gas
using wrong γ value for monoatomic vs diatomic
